questionText: Show that $y = \cot x$ is an odd function, and state what this tells you about the symmetry of its graph.

steps.explanation: A function is odd when replacing $x$ by $-x$ simply flips the sign of the output. Graphically this means rotational symmetry of order $2$ about the origin.

steps.description: Write cot of the negative angle
steps.explanation: We test the odd condition by feeding in $-x$, using the definition of cotangent as cosine over sine.

steps.description: Use the parities of cos and sin
steps.explanation: Cosine is even (a reflection in the $y$-axis leaves it unchanged) while sine is odd (it flips sign). These are standard facts from the unit circle.
Putting the two parities in, the numerator is unchanged and the denominator gains a minus sign.
Pulling the minus sign out front turns the ratio back into $\cot x$ with a negative sign attached.
We have shown exactly the odd condition $f(-x) = -f(x)$, so cotangent is an odd function.

steps.workingLatex: \text{graph has } 180^\circ \text{ rotational symmetry about the origin}
steps.explanation: Being odd means the curve looks the same after a half-turn about the origin: the branch on one side is the point-reflection of the branch on the other.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: $\cot(-x) = \dfrac{\cos(-x)}{\sin(-x)} = \dfrac{\cos x}{-\sin x} = -\cot x$, so $y=\cot x$ is odd; its graph has rotational symmetry of order $2$ about the origin.
